nativeRange.habitat: Found in shallow reef environments across the tropical Atlantic, Indo-Pacific, Caribbean, and Red Sea, typically attaching to rocky substrates and coral rubble at depths from the surface down to about 30 m.

avoidWith: aggressive herbivores that may graze on bubble algae

imageCandidates.notes: Philippe Bourjon; 1200x863. Sharp, well-centered single emerald-green glossy sphere with the classic star-reflection sheen — the cleanest single-subject shot of the diagnostic marble-like eyeball cell in situ.
Diego Delso; 2711x2711. Striking iridescent blue-purple cell amid white pulsing coral; sharp and vivid, shows the reflective metallic sheen well (some surface fouling).
Diego Delso; 4794x4794. Colourful cluster of several iridescent blue/purple/green cells together, showing how the alga aggregates on rock; sharp and high-resolution.

summary: Sailor's Eyeball is one of the largest known unicellular organisms — a single giant cell that can grow to roughly 5 cm across. It is sold as a display curiosity in the reef hobby but is widely considered a nuisance pest when it arrives uninvited on live rock.
careNotes:
Valonia ventricosa is one of the most remarkable organisms in a reef aquarium — a single cell that can grow to roughly 5 cm in diameter, about the size of a marble or small grape, earning it recognition as one of the largest unicellular organisms on Earth. Typical trade specimens measure roughly 1–5 cm across. It is worth noting that V. ventricosa is distinct from the more commonly cited reef pest Valonia macrophysa; the two are sometimes confused in the aquarium trade.

PEST CAVEAT (CRITICAL): Valonia ventricosa is widely regarded by reef keepers as a nuisance pest when it appears uninvited — arriving on live rock, coral frags, or other hitchhikers. In a well-lit reef with moderate nutrients it can spread rapidly, outcompeting corals for space. Most reef keepers actively remove it rather than cultivate it. Purchasing this species deliberately as a display specimen is a very different situation from an unwanted introduction on secondhand rock, but the distinction matters and the risks are real.

FRAGMENTATION WARNING: Do NOT pop or crush individual cells to remove them. Bursting the cell releases gametes and reproductive bodies that can seed a bloom throughout the tank. Manual removal should be done by gently prying whole cells from the substrate and removing them intact from the water column.

Attachment: Unlike red and brown algae, Valonia is a green unicellular alga that can generally be wedged or placed on rubble without adhesive. Its holdfast naturally anchors to hard substrate. Avoid burying in sand.

Water chemistry: Generally advised to keep within standard reef parameters — temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1,250–1,350 ppm, nitrate 5–20 ppm, phosphate 0.02–0.1 ppm. Weekly dosing of iron, iodine, and trace elements is advisable for long-term health of the macroalgae display. Avoid actinic-only spectrum lighting; full-spectrum LED is preferred.

Lighting: Medium intensity full-spectrum lighting is advised. Insufficient light may cause decline; excess nutrient load combined with high light can encourage faster spread.

Flow: Gentle to moderate flow is suitable; strong direct flow may dislodge holdfasts.
breedingNotes: Sailor's Eyeball reproduces primarily by fragmentation — individual cells can detach and settle on nearby substrate. Intentional propagation in the aquarium is generally inadvisable given pest concerns; if propagation is desired, carefully detach intact cells and relocate them rather than disrupting any existing ones. Physical disruption of the cell triggers gametophyte release (the dispersal of reproductive bodies), so cells should never be popped or crushed as a propagation method.
